title: Moments before mother-of-one dies after crashing off a high-thrill extreme slide ride in Colombia
text:
A mother died after crashing off a high-thrill extreme slide ride in Colombia on Thursday.

Cristel Camila Garcia, 28, suffered fatal injuries after falling 15ft to the ground when she crashed off an amusement park slide, which opened just two weeks ago, to the ground below.

Ms Garcia, who has a four-year-old daughter, was told 'don't be afraid' by a male worker at the top of the slide before she set off on the near-vertical drop.

She was pushed on a tyre by a female colleague on to the slide after asking: 'Will anyone be waiting for me?'

Workers had told Ms Garcia she would end up in a swimming pool below but she flew off the slide at the first bend.

Ms Garcia died on the way to the hospital after suffering severe injuries, including to her head.

She had been at an amusement park in Chinacota, a small town in Colombia's Norte de Santander department in the north-eastern region of the country, where she was visiting with her family.

A police investigation was ongoing and the attraction, which opened on February 21 according to Colombian media, had been closed until it was finalised.

Cristel Camila Garcia suffered fatal injuries after falling 15ft to the ground when she crashed off an amusement park slide, seen here at the top of the steep drop at the start of the attraction

After being pushed off the slide she quickly gained speed

She crashed off at the first bend in the slide and suffered fatal injuries

Ms Garcia was the mother of a four-year-old girl and was told 'don't be afraid' before riding the slide

The ride was closed while police investigated the fatal accident

Council chiefs said the attraction had been closed after initial investigations revealed it had not been operating with the proper license.

In a promotional video before Thursday's tragedy a female worker boasted: 'This is the only slide with a bend. Come and enjoy an extreme adventure with us.'

Attraction operator Entre Flores said: 'In light of this painful event, we would like to express our condolences and sincere sympathy to the family, friends and loved ones of Cristel Camila Garcia.

'We wholeheartedly join them in their grief during this difficult time.'

It said it had 'activated the emergency and assistance protocols provided for such cases and initial support was provided'.

'Entre Flores expresses its full willingness and commitment to cooperate with the competent authorities in the process of verifying and clarifying the facts,' it added.

'We are providing all the information required to ensure that the investigations proceed diligently.

'Our priority always has and always will be the wellbeing of those who visit us.'

A relative of the dead woman, whose full name was Yuris Cristel Camila Garcia Manrique, insisted no-one from Entre Flores had been in touch with them.

She said: 'So far this establishment has not contacted us at any time to say that they will assist us with the young woman's funeral or to say that they will respond.

'We reject what the lawyer for this establishment said, that they have contacted the family.

'At no point have they contacted the girl's mother or us.

'We demand that the investigation be carried out properly and that they answer for Camila's life, because she lost her life in an establishment where there was no security.'

title: The noxious nine: They broke their oath to the nation with acts such as rape, fraud and war crimes. Now Trump wants to revoke their citizenship
text:
When Senegalese immigrant Ndiaga Diagne raised his right hand and recited the Oath of Allegiance to become a US citizen, he pledged to defend 'the United States of America against all enemies'.

But 13 years later, he would become one.

The Islamic fanatic murdered three people and wounded a dozen others after opening fire at a bar in Austin, Texas, Sunday morning, following the US-Israeli airstrikes on Iran.

Wearing a 'Property of Allah' hoodie as he began the slaughter at Buford's Backyard Beer Garden, he had previously made no secret of his admiration for slain Iranian leader Ayatollah Ali Khameni in social media posts.

He was eventually shot dead at the scene by police.

But Diagne, 53, is far from alone in dishonoring the privilege of becoming a naturalized United States citizen. For many, the patriotic oath rings hollow even as they recite it.

Across the country, there are dozens of naturalized citizens who have gone on to become con artists, rapists, war criminals, killers, child sex offenders, and other perpetrators guilty of offenses that should disbar them from being an American.

Many have been slipping through the net through lies and subterfuge.

Senegal-born Ndiaga Diagne, 53, fatally shot three and wounded a dozen others in a shooting rampage at a Austin bar last Sunday. He became a naturalized US citizen in 2013

The gunman, who appeared to be an Islamic fanatic, was shot dead at the scene by police

But now President Donald Trump's immigration crackdown is accelerating efforts to strip citizenship from foreign-born criminals and those who allegedly lied in their applications.

Officials in the US Citizenship and Immigration Service have reportedly been told by the administration it wants to denaturalize between 100 to 200 per month in 2026 – compared to the 24 cases filed throughout the four years of the Biden administration.

Here, the Daily Mail spotlights the 'noxious nine' – the most shocking cases now at the center of the Justice Department's efforts to revoke citizenship.

Philippe Bien-Aime: The Haitian 'fraudster' mayor

Former North Miami mayor Philippe Bien-Aime is accused of using two identities to stay in the country and get citizenship

Bien-Aime was elected mayor of North Miami – which has a large Haitian-American population – in 2019. He is pictured with fellow city council members in November

Philippe Bien-Aime spent a term as mayor of North Miami, Florida. But he had 'illegally' entered the US from his native Haiti and then allegedly using two identities to stay in the country and get citizenship, according to the Department of Justice.

In a February court filing in Miami, the DOJ alleges that the 60-year-old so-called 'bigamist' used a photo-switched passport when he first arrived in 2001 under the name Jean Philippe Janvier.

He was ordered to be removed and he told authorities he was leaving for Haiti., according to the DOJ court paper.

However, he stayed and used Bien-Aime as a new name and married a US citizen to become a permanent resident, said the DOJ.

'The marriage was fraudulent and invalid because he was already married to a Haitian citizen,' the department added.

'After making numerous false and fraudulent statements in adjustment and naturalization proceedings, he naturalized in 2006.'

Bien-Aime's alleged immigration fraud was discovered through fingerprint comparisons of the two identities he used.

US Attorney Jason Quinones, for the Southern District of Florida, said: 'The fact that he later served as an elected mayor makes the alleged deception even more serious.'

In a statement, Bien-Aime's lawyer Peterson St Philippe said: 'We believe it is appropriate to address the allegations through the judicial process rather than through public commentary.'

'We trust any reporting will reflect that the matter remains unsolved and that no findings have been made,' he added.

Vladimir Volgaev: The Ukrainian gun runner

Volgaev, a native of Ukraine, smuggled huge caches of arms to his home country and Italy for two years from 2011 while living in Sarasota, Florida, the DOJ said.

But justice caught up with him in 2020 when he was sentenced to two years and nine months in federal prison for the gun running and scamming authorities for housing benefits.

He shipped more than 1,600 firearm components – including barrels and frames – from the US to Ukraine which were then turned into fully functioning handguns and rifles, the US District Court for the Middle District of Florida heard.

Volgaev, 74, was living in subsidized HUD housing at the time and lied about his personal finances, including the income he got from illicit firearms trafficking.

The Department of Justice said in its September 2025 denaturalization action: 'Volgaev misrepresented and concealed his crimes throughout his application for naturalization in 2014-15. He naturalized as a US citizen on January 11,2016.'

Assistant Attorney General Brett Shumate of the department's civil division said: 'The United States provided Volgaev with safety, housing, and citizenship, and he returned those gains with malice, including by defrauding one of the federal agencies that provided him benefits.'

Kemal Mrndzic: The Bosnian war prison camp guard

Kemal Mrndzic, pictured with family, did not disclose that he was a former guard at the notorious Celebici camp during the 1992-1995 Bosnian War in his application for citizenship, the DOJ said

Kemal Mrndzic covered up that he was a supervisor of guards at the notorious Celebici camp during the 1992-1995 Bosnian War when he applied for citizenship, the DOJ said in its December 2025 filing for denaturalization.

Guards at the camp routinely murdered and subjected Bosnian-Serb prisoners to beatings, sex assaults and starvation at the camp, the DOJ said in its civil complaint to revoke his citizenship.

Mrndzic, who was living in Swampscott, Massachusetts, was found guilty by a federal jury in October 2024 of criminal fraud in obtaining a naturalization certificate and passport. He was given 65 months in prison.

The court was told that 21 former detainees described Mrndzic as one of the most infamous guards at the camp and accused him of torturing and starving prisoners.

In his immigration interview, he falsely claimed he fled his homeland after being abused by Serb forces and could not return. He became a citizen in 2009.

Sammy Yetisen: The Bosnian war criminal

Sammy Yetisen, who arrived in the US as a Bosnian refugee in 1996, was granted US citizenship in 2001. Federal prosecutors say she hid the fact that she had engaged in human rights violations and war crimes in Bosnia

Sammy Yetisen came to the United States as a refugee in 1996 from Bosnia claiming she was persecuted as a Muslim, said the Justice Department.

She applied for US citizenship in 2001 and was granted it the following year.

However, she had omitted the fact that she had served in the notorious Bosnian Zulfikar special forces unit – and was involved in a massacre, the department added.

The DOJ said: 'Yetisen participated in the Trusina Massacre, a horrific attack on April 13, 1993 in which Yetisen and others lined up six unarmed Croat prisoners of war and civilians and executed them by firing squad.'

The former fighter was extradited to Bosnia in 2011 where she admitted war crimes and was given more than five years in prison, said the department. However, she returned to the US after completing her sentence.

An order revoking her citizenship was entered in 2023 in the US District Court for the District of Oregan and in July 2025 it was affirmed by the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals.

Luis Miguel Fernandez Gaviola: The Peruvian army commander charged with executing civilians

Fernandez served as a deputy commander in the 31st infantry division in the Peruvian military and was charged with eight extra-judicial killings while serving there, the DOJ said in its civil filing to strip citizenship.

But he concealed any kind of military service when applying for adjustment of status to become a permanent resident in 2003, the DOJ claims.

He also lied by saying he was never involved in political persecution when he subsequently applied for naturalization, according to the January 2026 filing in Miami, Florida.

The DOJ said it was alleged that 'in his role he commanded a unit that killed eight residents of the District of Pucara, Peru, in November 1989 due to their suspected ties to a rival political group.

'He then came to the US in 1992 'while hiding his horrific past'.

US Attorney Jason Reding Quinones said Fernandez had 'concealed material facts about his past, including involvement in grave human rights abuses'.

'While these are allegations only, our responsibility is clear,' added Quinones. 'When credible evidence shows that citizenship may have been procured through fraud or concealment, the Department of Justice will act to protect the integrity of our immigration system and the rule of law.

Fernandez could not be contacted for comment and he has no attorney of record.

Marieva Briceno: The Venezuelan Medicare fraudster

Briceno, originally from Venezuela, scammed more than $5.4 million from Medicare than obtained US citizenship by hiding her crimes from immigration officials, said the DOJ.

She owned three 'purported medical clinics' around Detroit, Michigan that paid people to have unnecessary tests and procedures. These were then turned into fraudulent claims.

Briceno applied for US citizenship in December 2009 and denied ever committing a crime for which she had not been arrested.

However, the US Attorney's Office for the Southern District of Florida charged her with health care fraud in September 2011. She pleaded guilty in March the following year and was sentenced to 60 months in prison, said the department.

The DOJ filed a complaint to revoke her citizenship in in August 2025 and a judge entered the order in November.

Elliott Duke: The Brit caught with child porn

UK-born Elliott Duke became an American citizen and enlisted in the army before being convicted of distributing child sex abuse material

British citizen Duke became an American after enlisting in the US Army and listed only a speeding ticket on his citizenship application in 2012, said the DOJ.

However, while he was serving in Germany earlier that year, he had began receiving and distributing child sex abuse material.

At his citizenship interview in January 2013 he denied he'd ever committed a crime for which he had not been arrested.

Four months later he was arrested in Leesville, Louisiana, and charged.

Detectives found 168 videos and 187 still images on the now 41-year-old's computer depicting young boys and girls engaged in sexual activity and bestiality.

He was convicted in 2014 and sentenced to 20 years at a federal court in Lake Charles, Louisiana.

In February 2025, the DOJ filed to strip him of his citizenship and a judge ordered it in June.

Gurmeet Singh: The Indian kidnapper and rapist

Former New York taxi driver Gurmeet Singh kidnapped and raped a passenger at knifepoint in 2012. Prosecutors say he had already become a US citizen after concealing the crime

Former New York taxicab driver Singh, who came to the US from India, kidnapped and raped a young woman passenger then 'concealed these acts' through his naturalization process, the DOJ said in a February 2026 filing to strip citizenship.

He became an American in October 2011. But a year later the former cabbie, now 54, was arrested for the horrific attack.

'After his passenger fell asleep in the backseat. he drove her to a side street and the passenger awoke to find Singh on top of her with a knife to her throat, telling her to stop resisting if she wanted to live,' said the DOJ.

'Singh then bound and gagged her, blindfolded her, removed her clothes and raped her.' He was given 20 years in prison in 2014.

Attorney General Pam Bondi is quoted as saying in the DOJ's information on the case that her department 'will continue to strip citizenship from those who commit heinous crimes and conceal them during the naturalization process'.

Nicholas Eshun: The Ghanaian sex offender

Ghana-born Nicholas Eshun is a former US Marine who was court-martialed after sending explicit messages to someone he believed wasa 14-year-old girl in 2015

Eshun, from Ghana, won US citizenship two years after enlisting in the US Marine Corp in 2011.

But in 2015 he 'tarnished the honor' of the service by exchanging lewd messages with someone he thought was a 14-year-old girl, said the DOJ in its December 2025 denaturalization action in Maryland.

In fact, Eshun was communicating while serving overseas with an undercover officer of the Naval Criminal Investigative Service.

He was court-martialed and kicked out of the Marines.

Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche said: 'As alleged, this defendant betrayed the uniform, abused the trust of this nation and targeted who he believed was a child.

'Citizenship is not a shield for criminals who never deserved it in the first place.'

title: Man arrested for setting Tacoma apartment on fire overnight Sunday, police say
text:

Fire crews found a large garbage pile burning outside the front door of an apartment, according to a Tacoma Fire Department social media post.

Advertisement Advertisement

“Fire burned the exterior of the building, and smoke filled the unit,” the social media post reads. “Crews pulled one resident out of the building and treated them at the scene.”

Tacoma police also arrested the arson suspect at the scene, Sgt. John Correa said Sunday.

title: Officials say 3 arrested in Peru nightclub bombing as number of injured rises to 44
text:
LIMA, Peru (AP) — Authorities in Peru on Sunday said they have arrested three people following the deadly bombing of a nightclub as the number of those injured rose to 44.

The explosion occurred early Saturday at the Dalí nightclub in the city of Trujillo in La Libertad region, which has the largest gold-producing area in Peru and is rife with extortion and illegal mining.

La Libertad Regional Health Management confirmed that those injured rose from 33 to 44, including three minors between the ages of 16 and 17.

Advertisement Advertisement

At least 10 patients have been discharged, while four others remain in serious condition, said Gerardo Florián, executive director of the Trujillo Health Network.

Municipal officials said they shuttered the club for 30 days because it violated an operating hours limit and an ordinance prohibiting minors from entering the establishment.

The club did not immediately respond to a message seeking comment.

The police press office confirmed to The Associated Press that three people had been arrested in the case without providing more details. Video broadcast on local media showed the suspects handcuffed and being led away by police.

Advertisement Advertisement

Saturday’s explosion is the latest to hit La Libertad.

In 2025, the region experienced 286 explosions, 136 of which occurred in the city of Trujillo, according to official figures.

In January of that year, an explosive device detonated in a prosecutor’s office building in Trujillo, and in August and September, two explosions left more than twenty people injured and damaged dozens of homes.

title: Beloved pizza chain files 4th Chapter 11 bankruptcy in a year
text:
The pizza dining sector has dealt with an industry downturn over the last two years, forcing major pizza chains to close 100s of locations and, in some cases, file for bankruptcy.

Among the economic issues, restaurants blame fierce competition, rising labor and food costs, and high lease rates that have required several companies to launch restructurings.

A popular San Francisco-based pizza chain has filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy for the fourth time in a year, underscoring the intense financial pressure facing independent restaurants.

Pizza chain Fiorella files for Chapter 11 bankruptcy for the fourth time in less than a year.r.classen/Shutterstock · r.classen/Shutterstock

Fiorella files for bankruptcy again

Wood-fired pizza restaurant chain Fiorella, with four locations in San Francisco, has filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy for the fourth time to reorganize another location and continue operating.

Fiorella's Noe Valley location at 4042 24th Street, known as Project Pizza Noe LLC, filed its Chapter 11 petition in the U.S. Bankruptcy Court for the Northern District of California on March 6, 2026, according to PacerMonitor.

The Noe Valley filing follows three Chapter 11 filings by Fiorella's Project Pizza parent companies in 2025.

Project Pizza Polk LLC, which operates the chain’s pizza and Italian restaurant location Fiorella Polk at 2238 Polk Street, filed its Subchapter V petition on July 2, 2025, in the Northern District of California, listing $100,000 to $500,000 in assets and $1 million to $10 million in liabilities in its petition.

More bankruptcies:

Affiliate Project Pizza LLC, which operates the pizza chain's flagship location, Fiorella Clement, located at 2339 Clement Street, filed a Subchapter V petition on May 20, 2025, listing $50,000 to $100,000 in assets and $1 million to $10 million in liabilities in its petition.

Another affiliate, Project Pizza Sunset LLC, filed a Chapter 11 Subchapter V petition on April 1, 2025, on behalf of its Fiorella Sunset location at 1240 9th Avenue.

The restaurant chain did not reveal reasons for filing the bankruptcy petitions.

Fiorella has operated for 10 years

Fiorella's partners Boris Nemchenok and Brandon Gillis opened the first location on Clement Street in 2016, followed by the Russian Hill location on Polk Street in 2019. The partners opened the Sunset location in 2021 and Noe Valley restaurant in 2024.

Related: McDonald’s offers customers free meal in exchange for this one thing

Other pizza chains that filed for bankruptcy in 2025 included Bertucci’s Restaurants, which filed for Chapter 11 protection on April 24, 2025, and Backdraughts, filing on July 23, 2025.

title: Foreign banks' credit card base shrinks as local rivals step up
text:
Synopsis

Data released by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) shows that several foreign lenders saw a decline in their active card base in 2025, even as the overall credit card market continued to grow rapidly, driven largely by private sector banks. Among major foreign issuers, American Express saw its active cards decline to 1.334 million in January 2026 from 1.453 million a year earlier, a drop of 118,000 cards.

title: Fox News says sorry for airing wrong clip of Donald Trump: 'They did not want to show Trump wearing his hat'
text: Fox News apologized for airing the wrong clip of President Donald Trump when the president honored the six US service members killed in Iran. During our coverage of yesterday’s dignified transfer, we inadvertently aired video from an older dignified transfer instead of the ceremony that took place yesterday. We deeply regret the air and extend our respect and condolences to the service members’ families. title: Unclaimed Baggage Center reveals the most incredible and bizarre items packed by travelers
text:
If you’ve ever wondered what happens to luggage that never makes it to its destination, you’re in for a treat. Unclaimed Baggage, the nation’s only retailer of lost airline luggage, has released its third annual “Found Report,” revealing the most unusual, valuable and downright bizarre items discovered in orphaned suitcases over the past year. From everyday essentials to high-end luxury items, the report offers a rare glimpse into travel trends and consumer habits across America.

The Reality of Lost Luggage

In 2025, nearly 10 billion passengers traveled by air globally, with an average of 27 million travelers flying every day. While over 99.9% of checked luggage reaches its rightful owner, a tiny fraction gets left behind. After a 90-day search, airlines officially declare these bags “orphaned,” compensating travelers for lost belongings before sending the items to Unclaimed Baggage in Scottsboro, Alabama. There, the contents are sold, recycled, or donated to charitable causes through their Reclaimed for Good program.

Bryan Owens, owner of Unclaimed Baggage, says, “After more than 55 years of reclaiming the lost and rejected for good, we often believe we’ve seen it all. But then we uncover something like a matching set of Samurai swords, a fully-assembled robot, a Dolce & Gabbana jeweled jacket, or gold-plated golf clubs and we are reminded of why the annual ‘Found Report’ exists.”

A Glimpse into American Travel Trends

The 2026 report highlights some of the most fascinating discoveries from lost luggage:

Advertisement Advertisement

Top Finds: From a beekeeper’s suit to a custom set of diamond-studded grills, the report underscores just how unpredictable what travelers pack can be.

Most Valuable Finds: Luxury items abound, including a $35,000 Rolex, a $10,800 Chanel handbag, and a $12,500 Balenciaga leather jacket.

Curated Discoveries: Categories include cutting-edge gadgets, historical artifacts, fashion items, musical instruments, sports memorabilia, coin collections, and even pop culture collectibles like autographs from Saquon Barkley and Paul “Bear” Bryant.

Travel Trends: Viral fashion items like the Trader Joe’s tote bag and unusual gold pieces show what consumers are following in real time.

Advertisement Advertisement

Found Stories: Some items tell stories of mystery or humor, a suitcase packed with rat poison, a set of fake human bones, or a treasured religious memento, revealing the quirky and human side of travel.

Matt Owens, SVP of Commercial Strategy, adds, “The trends identified are just as fascinating as the items themselves. The ‘Found Report’ provides a detailed overview of how our habits, values, and priorities are evolving. Over time, the report will serve as a cultural time capsule, helping us understand where we’ve been and where we’re going as a society.”

Travel Tips from Lost Luggage

Beyond quirky finds, the Found Report offers practical tips for travelers seeking to avoid lost luggage:

Clearly label bags inside and out

Keep valuables and essentials in carry-on luggage

Use luggage trackers and smart locks

Advertisement Advertisement

My wife and I almost always put Apple AirTags in our bags, so when the airline loses our luggage, it’s no big deal. However, last month I forgot to put an AirTag in my carry-on and left it at security. It took almost a month to get it back, but if I had an AirTag in it, it likely would have been returned the next day. Here’s that story.

title: The $6.7 Billion Statement That Stops Chord Energy Investors Cold
text:
Quick Read

Chord Energy (CHRD) returned $6.7B since 2021, more than its $6.9B market cap, and is up 31% YTD. Revenue of $1.17B beat by 15%, EPS of $1.28 missed by 16.88%, and 2026 free cash flow guidance is $700M.

Oil prices at $71 vs Chord Energy’s $64 guidance assumption create upside to the $700M free cash flow target, supported by 22% lower F&D costs from long lateral conversions.

The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 AI stocks. Get them here FREE.

Chord Energy (NASDAQ:CHRD) CEO Danny Brown had a clear message on the February 26, 2026 earnings call: the company has returned more money to shareholders since 2021 than it is currently worth. That is not a typo.

"Since 2021, Chord Energy has returned $6.7 billion of capital to shareholders, which is particularly impressive given it is higher than our current market cap."

With a market cap sitting around $6.9 billion, that statement lands hard. Brown was not just touting a number. He was making the case that Chord has been a capital return machine while growing the business, keeping leverage low, and building a deeper inventory than it started with.

READ: The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 AI stocks

What the Numbers Actually Show

The headline financials were mixed. Revenue came in at $1.17 billion, beating estimates by nearly 15%, but adjusted EPS of $1.28 missed the $1.54 consensus by 16.88%. The culprit was straightforward: crude oil realizations fell to $56.90 per barrel from $63.59 a year ago. Lower prices hit the bottom line even as volumes held firm.

On the operational side, Chord delivered. Oil volumes hit 153.0 MBopd, at the high end of guidance, while capital came in below the low end. For full year 2025, Brown pointed to something more structural: $160 million of run-rate free cash flow improvement from controllable items, representing 23% of estimated 2026 free cash flow.

The Long Lateral Story Is the Real Thesis

Brown set a goal of converting 80% of Chord's inventory to long laterals by year-end 2025. They hit it early.

"Chord's future F&D cost on a company level has trended 22% lower over the past few years, clearly demonstrating that things are going in a positive direction."

This is not just an efficiency story. Lower finding and development costs mean more previously marginal inventory is now economic. Brown put it plainly: "Some things that we always thought were inventory are just now better inventory than we had before, and then some things before that would not have made sense for us to drill now have really compelling returns."
